sentences.text: We came to that barrack, more than 500 people, more than 500 people.
Everybody-- first of all, there was nowhere to-- assigned a place where you should be, sleep, or move.
Was empty boards, completely empty boards.
And excuse me, the cockroaches was running around in front of you.
And we had to lay down.
No covers to cover themself, nothing behind.
I mean, I'm talking about mattresses.
This was nothing.
When we-- we got that food, the soup, what they're supposed to be for this people, which they would come back from work, from [NON-ENGLISH], so we had that soup.
When they started, they said, [INAUDIBLE]-- you know, for a day, for almost two days not to eat, everybody was pushing themselves to get that little soup.
So we had the little soup.
And we ate up the soup, and we went to lay down.
There was a kapo there, an assigned kapo from before, from this group, the kapo remained there.
And they start to chase us.
You know, I-- it's like even dogs you cannot chase like that, but they-- they-- they-- [MAKES NOISE] knocking over the head, and over the feet, and over the body.
Go!
Fast.
Fast.
Fast!
More fast.
What?
Lay down, and don't stay in their-- under the way.
It was evening already, because we came in, this was late afternoon.
And they're supposed to come back from work.
And they came back from work, and they made the selection there also.
So the rest, we were-- so we laid down.
In the morning, we got up.
This appell outside.
They put us together, and they start to-- to divide it-- the groups-- this group here, and this group here, this group-- and I was assigned to the [NON-ENGLISH].
We were about 25 people, young ones, and also with a gruppenfuhrer.
And they took us out to work.
Every day, in the morning we got up.
The soup consisted just of a little water with a-- a little potato, either sometimes a little black coffee.
And every-- every day and evening, for-- [INAUDIBLE] they gave us a piece of bread, a half a pound of bread [INAUDIBLE].
I smoked that time.
I was a smoker.
I remember, I take my portion from that bread, and I gave it away for somebody, he should give me a cigarette.
A cigarette.
But this is not-- this is not the-- the-- the point of it.
The point is we had to go start it-- the daily routine from the work and what.
And at night, there came to that barrack-- and if they touched somebody at that leg, because he was lame, they have to go out with them.
Every day, there is less.
They took out a few people to be shot, to be shot.
I would-- then, when I found this out, that I was lame, every night I was laying so-- you know, together, when mine legs was almost with my mouth.
They should not touch mine-- then, every day was.
Then, some women was also there, not in the same barrack, some other barrack.
And we heard the rumors in the morning that they got some women there, raped them, and they shot them, and-- they didn't went back to there.
So we were there in that-- I was there in that barrack-- well, almost for six weeks.
We chopped wood.
And we brought-- and this-- this was consist of the-- that work, in chopping wood, you know?
In-- in the-- in the forest there.
Out of the camp.
Out of the camp, and walked, you know, about-- it's the place where the graves were there, you know?
Walked about 300 feet, 300 feet, maybe more.
Well, somewhere around there, to the [NON-ENGLISH] and we were working.
And the SS man took us there to work.
I had-- had Stubel.
I had [INAUDIBLE] I-- I have the whole-- I have the whole name from-- a list of-- you wouldn't believe it.
I have the names from all the-- I have it somewhere.
I-- my mind-- [LAUGHS]
Was a kapo.
No.
[INAUDIBLE] it's-- was a Jewish boy.
Or he was not-- he was not a kapo from that camp.
He was a gruppenfuhrer from that group.
You know?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yes.
I don't-- I-- he-- I know he was from-- from Sokolov.
I know the-- the town from where he was there.
I don't remember the-- the-- a Jewish-- I-- a boy.
That's all right. yeah.
Yes.
We brought it to the-- to the camp, you see, because the-- the cooks, they had to cook the soup there.
They didn't have electricity with-- they had to have that wood, you know?
The outside from the camp, when we went out from that camp, was a-- a-- field.
Going to that-- to that forest was also about 300 feet, more than 300 feet there, going over to that.
We walked from-- we passed by the holtzplatz, you know?
The holtzplatz is the place where we brought that wood.
We chopped up wood in pieces and put them together.
And this was-- this was-- we had to pass by this-- this place, go out a gate, which was a guard there.
And then, under their jurisdiction-- jurisdiction from that SS man-- the [NON-ENGLISH], they take it to us there.
We were there.
Who went there.
Outside was a fence, yeah, a double fence.
Wire.
Wire.
No, there was not.
This-- this fence was not electric.
It was the--
Barbed wire.
That's right.
Double fence, you see, because it were-- I don't want to come over to this.
Yeah, barbed, double fence.
And you know, we worked, and we came back in the morning.
Every day, the same thing.
Food?
Tools?
Axes, and, you know, the saws, this kind of wood tool to chop that wood, you know?
Being-- yeah?
Yes.
No, we didn't see no Polish people.
There's no people living around there.
One time we were there, standing and working, and we heard a crying, cry, crying, crying.
And it comes closer, and closer, and closer, that crying from children.
They were crying.
All of a sudden, we turned around.
We saw bringing a group of gypsies, a group of 200, 300 people.
That's not just-- and don't forget, we didn't chop just the wood, we had to dig the graves also.
That's a whole line.
We have to see some people were digging that-- the-- the graves for themself, you see?
For themself, they was digging the grave.
And some of them--
We hear the crying, and crying and crying, and closer, and closer.
We saw gypsies carrying the babies with the-- on the bundles, on the-- on their backs.
And they brought them over to this forest, to this graves, which they were digged up.
And they tell them everything to take away.
And they would-- A half an hour.
Didn't took a half an hour.
They shot them, all of them, in the-- into the-- the-- into the graves.
After the shooting, they are told, the-- the Ukrainian told us we should go pick up that bread what they have and take-- take from them, that we should have some bread to eat.
Yes.
What?
The Ukrainian SS.
The SS-- they worked together as a-- as a team, although most of them, Ukrainian.
The-- a job like this, it was the Ukrainian that did it.
And they shot them.
What can you do?
You cannot do anything.
[INAUDIBLE] The-- the fact is, you're never taking the-- the gypsies over to that-- to the graves with the children on their back and-- and finish them out there.
I'd been there in that camp, in that Taunton barrack.
I was there about six, seven weeks.
During the six, seven weeks, there was every days less, less, and less people remained in that barrack that would come out.
They shot them.
You see?
That's no--
And that other-- the other-- so we had-- let me show you something.
I didn't have-- I don't have to work too hard, [INAUDIBLE].
This is Treblinka.
You're coming in from the gate, from here.
You come in from the gate here.
This is the administrations place.
The administration from the SS, they have here.
This is the place for the people who are working.
I'd been-- and that Taunton barrack is one of this-- I don't know how-- all of this barracks, you see, was the Taunton barrack.
When they took them out-- you see the double-- you see, this in the double fence.
And this side was not a double fence.
Here, is the double fence, you see?
We went out one morning to work.
And a guy was standing behind these two wires.
And he stood-- but we know that he's going to be shot in half an hour, an hour.
He gonna-- he cannot get out from there alive, absolutely.
Accidentally.
I knew that man.
He's first from [NON-ENGLISH].
[INAUDIBLE]
[INAUDIBLE]
And he started to cry, please, give me a piece of bread before I'm going to die.
I want to feel that taste from a piece of bread before I'm going to die.
We were standing behind this wire, this double wire.
I mean, who could help him?
Nobody can help.
You asked me about this.
Here was a-- here was the-- the laundry.
See?
Everything is marked here, what it-- everything.
What is here?
Is it marked?
What is this?
What is this?
What is this?
And we were here in this [INAUDIBLE].
This was a Polish barrack.
Polacks were there in this barrack.
But they didn't-- didn't shot them.
They worked there.
They did their work, their own [INAUDIBLE].
Here, was the latrines, the toilets.
This there, you see this there.
Was not a toilet to go in.
I don't have to tell you.
Sit down, a whole group.
And we-- you know?
And down here started the handwerker.
You know what a handwerker means?
A handwerker is, let's say, I'm-- in Poland, it was-- if I was self-sufficient.
I had a little shop, and some people work for me there, I worked with that people.
It's a-- it's small-- a small businessman.
That's a handwerker.
Or he's got some-- You see, here, the handwerker.
Handwerkers consisted of shoemakers-- Tailors?
Tailors.
Yeah.
All this kind of work.
You see this, over here, you see, here, let's say, was the barracks.
This, too, was where they were sleeping.
This is the arbeitz places where they were there, you see?
When I talked about the Otwock, when I told you about Otwock, it's-- the group what was from Otwock were there.
They was the first one what they grabbed.
They to told them to send them to Treblinka.
They build that [INAUDIBLE] Treblinka.
So they were working in this shop.
I knew them.
They knew me also, from Otwock.
One Sunday morning, I was assigned from this group, from this Taunton barrack, assigned to go over there, in-- not-- not by myself, you see?
With a few other boys, and bring over something-- either some bricks, either something.
I don't remember exactly what, we bring it over there.
And I knew that they are there in this shop, so I-- you know, I hide myself.
And I jumped into the shop, and I looked at them, and they looked at me.
And I said, please, [NON-ENGLISH] Help me.
You know?
If not today, it's tomorrow I'm going to be dead.
He said, what can we-- we cannot do anything.
So one guy-- he's-- that's Katz.
His name was Katz.
He said, wait a minute.
You-- don't-- don't move out from here.
Don't move out from here.
He gave me a-- I don't know how you say [INAUDIBLE] to shave the wood.
A-- a plane?
Yeah, to shave the wood, you know?
And he gave me in the hand to me, put-- put me in-- aside.
And I should stay and work.
And he said, don't-- don't say anything.
And I was working.
He was helping me, until the-- about 4 or 5 o'clock, when the appell is there.
I have to be there on the other side.
Then I finished it to this, and I went out-- I went back.
The next morning, the SS came in to that Taunton barrack and to take out the people to the appell.
So you know, I was there.
And I said-- they said, who works there on the-- he asked them-- who belongs to that handwerker?
I picked up my arm.
Are you telling the truth?
Yeah, I worked there, I said, yesterday.
He said, [INAUDIBLE].
I worked there.
And he sent me over there.
He sent me over there.
And I came in.
They said, [INAUDIBLE] Lanz is going to come in, and he's going to see you.
He's going to kill you on the place.
Lanz was the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] from the-- from all the factories what was there.
He took care of that.
I don't-- I said, I don't care [INAUDIBLE].
Let me stay here.
You know?
It's-- I will stay over there another day.
Then Lanz came in.
Lanz came in.
And the meister, the Jews, the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] said, Herr Lanz, we need-- we need some more people here to the arbeit.
Do I have-- I was not by myself.
There was another two guys, also.
The same thing.
Well, we have here three [NON-ENGLISH].
What shall I do with the three [NON-ENGLISH]?
Leave them here.
Then, I know I'm going to live.
Meanwhile, [INAUDIBLE].
Leave them here.
So after the day's work, they said to the gruppenfuhrer-- I don't know who was there-- take them to the barrack.
Because there, had different clothing.
You see?
They have already a jacket, a little jacket, with a mark here, handwerker, you see?
With a little-- yeah.
Take him to the barrack.
We were afraid.
They were going to take us to the barracks.
We know we're going to the [INAUDIBLE].
They took us to the barrack.
They gave us a shirt and different clothing.
I was wearing a pair of pants-- I fell out from them.
They were like that-- they didn't fell out from me.
No, I fell out from them.
And they sent us back to the-- to that barrack.
Not to the Taunton barrack, but to the handwerker.
And I remained.
This is the way how I remained there alive.
And I was still alive.
That's the-- the-- I came in.
This was before the holiday.
That's what I'm saying.
That's before the holidays.
I was assigned and-- I was laying already under there-- not a bed.
It's also the-- [INAUDIBLE] you had a little pillow, and they have blankets to cover themself.
And some straw was laying in behind.
You see?
It's different.
I was accidentally was assigned to sleep.
The next-- the next one from me, he was the baker, the Jewish baker, from Treblinka.
And he was from Warsaw, a very known bakery in Warsaw.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I'm going to remind myself the name of him.
And I was laying with him.
A lot of people-- the-- the-- the boys, they ask him, give me a piece of bread.
You know?
You have bread.
Give me a piece of bread.
I was laying with him about two weeks, three weeks, and I never ask him for a piece of bread.
One morning, I got up from-- he-- he left early.
I got up in the morning, and make my bed.
I take a look.
It's-- it's a piece-- a half a bread is laying under my pillow.
What did he-- I-- after he left, I usually did for him the bed, yeah, every morning.
You see?
Every morning, I make it, straighten it out.
And I put the pillow down.
The name-- it's-- I want to say the name, and it's hard for me.
But he-- so helped me a lot.
And the four weeks later, not even four weeks, but two, three weeks later, they liquidated that whole barrack.
That barrack, they liquidated completely.
They took out the people, they shot-- they shot them.
That's all right.
No more the Taunton barrack.
No people-- no more people bringing in, because the ghetto was liquidated.
They didn't have people from where to bring them in.
So this remained open, you know?
A open place.
And I worked with them together.
I worked in-- and I had that-- the soup what he's supposed to get every-- every day.
Well, he gave an order that-- for who gave out the soup, then I should pick up the soup for him also.
So I had soup, and I have bread.
[LAUGHS] Everybody was around me.
And these people-- you know, it's easy to say, the people, they were so hungry, if the farm sometimes a horse died, they took the horse and would cut it up.
And they cooked it there.
And you found a piece of that meat in that soup, is wonderful.

Now, what I'm trying to bring out is, these people-- there were some people-- let's say, if you had that-- a bone found in the soup, and you were sitting and sucking out that bone and throw it away to the other guy, what he was so hungry, he picked it up, that bone, and he started to sucking it.
And he threw it away.
The third one comes over.
And the-- this-- this is the people that died first.
They died.
So you saw, every day, dying, and dying, from that-- from that hunger.
And they didn't have what to eat.
I was a king.
I had a piece of bread and a little soup.
And I gave it to friends also.
So we-- I was there.
And we went to the shop every morning.
There was no more Taunton barrack.
What were you making in the shop?
What were you doing?
All kind of carpentry.
They-- that-- now, that-- but the end, we realized that they made the coffers, you know?
Boxes to prepare themself to put in everything in the boxes and to take out from there.
You see?
Well, they had their belongings, and the belongings from the-- from the-- from the lager, from the camp.
And then, they were close with the other camp, you know?
The Ukrainians were close with the other one, that you-- there's gold.
And-- and-- and-- everything was laying on the-- in the-- in the Treblinka where they burned them.
They were on the ground.
You know?
Which area?
Lanz.
Lanz, that's right.
One day, before I-- I-- one-- this is very characteristic-- one day, we went out to [NON-ENGLISH], work the [NON-ENGLISH].
Coming back from work was a sturmfuher there with the name-- we called him the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH] A older man.
He was an older man.
Now, he was a completely murderer.
If you-- you-- you don't-- you cannot understand it, you know?
You-- he-- he-- when you-- you hear this-- I told this in the front of the court also, whenever I was called to-- We came back from work, and we went into the camp, not the-- outside.
And this-- he was standing outside.
And he made that appell with all the-- and he said, you better watch out.
You shouldn't catch a cold.
He said that to us.
Talking this, he took out five people.
Come here.
Come here.
He laid them down, and he shot them.
He just said to us we should watch out not to catch a cold, and he take out five people.
There was another [INAUDIBLE] I was already in the handwerker.
Two young boys-- we called them-- one, we called the [NON-ENGLISH].
The other one we call the [NON-ENGLISH].
The name-- these names.
And the [NON-ENGLISH] was there, a young 15-year-old, 16-year-old boy.
He cleaned the toilets, you know?
He took it down to the-- that's why we called him-- we called him [NON-ENGLISH].
These two boys decided they're going to run away from camp.
And they run away.
Two days later, they brought them back.
We had to stay and watch how they hanged them.
They make them-- you know-- a place, and they hung them during the day.
These two boys, they hanged.
The charge of-- a Ukrainian.
An SS man.
Lanz was in charge of that-- everything around.
That's what I'm trying to tell you.
I'm coming over to that, Lanz.
Lanz was-- and if we looked at-- looked through the windows-- you see, there was-- the window, we were here.
And the hanging came on the-- the other side.
And we looked at-- we saw that they hung them.
They pulled them by their legs.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] He was dead already, they pulled them.
He couldn't have-- he did something, you know, where he couldn't understand it.
[INAUDIBLE] run away from here.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So you know, and I was there in that shop working.
Till one day-- this was in 19-- by the end of '44.
[INAUDIBLE] '44, when we are escaped.
I was working there for nine months.
I'd been working in the shop.
That's-- one morning, we heard that something is going to happen here in that camp.
This is in the time when we heard the Russian army already starting to advance on Poland.
They took already Lublin.
They took some-- you heard.
People, which they were working in the offices by the-- by the SS, the German Jews, they were working in the offices from the-- the SS, they heard the radio.
They came back to the camps.
They said, oh, the Russians, they're in Lublin.
[INAUDIBLE].
